coredata.dc:title: The effect of indium tin oxide nanoparticles (ITO NPs) incorporated with ZnO NPs based on structural, optical and application for flexible dye sensitized solar cells (FDSSCs)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Etefa, Habtamu Fekadu
Dejene, Francis Birhanu
coredata.dc:description: This study aimed to investigate the impact of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) doped with indium tin oxide nanoparticles (ITO NPs) on polymer-based dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The ZnO NPs and ITO NPs were synthesized using the sol-gel and co-precipitation methods, respectively. They were then characterized and applied for dye-sensitized solar cells of a polymer-based substrate. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) analysis revealed the presence of hexagonal structures and small spherical nanoparticles. The mean diameter of the ZnO NPs was found to be 15.22 ± 0.11 nm, while the ZnO NPs doped with ITO NPs (ZnO NPs/ITO NPs) had a mean diameter of 14.54 ± 0.16 nm. The effects of the ITO NPs on the ZnO NPs were evaluated, and it was found that these ITO NPs enhanced the performance of the flexible electrode DSSCs. After the addition of ITO NPs, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the ZnO NPs was boosted to be three times higher than that of the ZnO NPs DSSCs without ITO NPs. Similarly, the ultraviolet (UV) band exhibited a red shift, resulting in a lower band gap and reduced charge transfer resistance, which can enhance the PCE of the DSSCs. The polyethylene terephthalate (PET)/indium tin oxide (ITO) (PET/ITO) substrate conductive polymer showed positive outcomes for DSSCs. The PCE value for ZnO NPs was 2.198 ± 0.14 %, while for ZnO NPs/ITO NPs, it was 6.680 ± 0.12 %. This work achieved remarkable and competitive performance when compared to a solid (indium tin oxides/glass)-based substrate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO NPs
ITO NPs
Polymer electrode
DDSCs
ZnO NPs/ITO NPs

(B) Preparation of ITO nanoparticles by precipitation method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Walter Sisulu University (WSU) for its support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (A) (a) ZnO NPs and (b) ZnO NPs@ITO NPs, SEM image of) (c) ZnO NPs and (d) ZnO NPs@ITO NPs (e) size distribution histograms of ZnO NPs (f) size distribution histograms of ZnO NPs@ITO NPs (g) EDS of ZnO NPs (h) EDS of ZnO NPs@ITO NPs.
XRD of (A) ZnO NPs at different temperature (B) ZnO NPs@ITO NPs at different doped mass of ITO NPs.

Uv–Vis of (A) ZnO NPs at different temperature (B) ZnO NPs (4500C) at different doped mass of ITO NPs (C) Band gap of ZnO NPs at different temperature (D) Correlation of Band gap and temperature (E) Band gap of ZnO NPs at different doped mass of ITO NPs (F) Correlation of Band gap and mass doped.
X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) image of ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs@ITO NPs.

(A) Room Temperature Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ZnO NPs at different annealing temperature (B) Commission International del’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram of ZnO NPs at different annealing temperatures (C) Low temperature Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of ZnO NPs/ITO NPs(10 wt%) at 4500Cannealing temperature.
I–V performance of (A) (a) ZnO NPs (b) ZnO NPs/ITO NPs (5 wt %) (c) ZnO NPs/ITO NPs (15 wt %) (d) ZnO NPs/ITO NPs (10 wt %) (B) I–V parameters calculated.
Sheet resistance of undoped and ITO NPs doped ZnO NPs films at various (at %).
EDS (elemental composition Wt % and at %) and band gap of ZnO NPs, ZnO NPs/ITO NPs.
XRD diffraction peaks values and d-spacing corresponding its indices, of the ZnO NP obtained by the precipitation synthesis at different annealing temperature.

row.entry: ZnO NPs (d-spacing, indices and 2theta) at different annealing temperature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The I–V performance of ZnO NP@ITO NP based on the FDSSC substrate as the photocathode at different Wt% of ITO NPs deposited on ZnO NPs.

row.entry: D/t Wt% of ITO NPs deposited on ZnO NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Evaluation of the PCE effectiveness of flexible electrodes constructed of different materials for DSSCs with regards to substrates that are commercial and noncommercial.

head.ce:title: The effect of indium tin oxide nanoparticles (ITO NPs) incorporated with ZnO NPs based on structural, optical and application for flexible dye sensitized solar cells (FDSSCs)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Walter Sisulu University, Private Bag X-1, 5117, Mthatha, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Walter Sisulu University, Private Bag X-1, 5117, Mthatha, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aimed to investigate the impact of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) doped with indium tin oxide nanoparticles (ITO NPs) on polymer-based dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The ZnO NPs and ITO NPs were synthesized using the sol-gel and co-precipitation methods, respectively. They were then characterized and applied for dye-sensitized solar cells of a polymer-based substrate. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) analysis revealed the presence of hexagonal structures and small spherical nanoparticles. The mean diameter of the ZnO NPs was found to be 15.22 ± 0.11 nm, while the ZnO NPs doped with ITO NPs (ZnO NPs/ITO NPs) had a mean diameter of 14.54 ± 0.16 nm. The effects of the ITO NPs on the ZnO NPs were evaluated, and it was found that these ITO NPs enhanced the performance of the flexible electrode DSSCs. After the addition of ITO NPs, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the ZnO NPs was boosted to be three times higher than that of the ZnO NPs DSSCs without ITO NPs. Similarly, the ultraviolet (UV) band exhibited a red shift, resulting in a lower band gap and reduced charge transfer resistance, which can enhance the PCE of the DSSCs. The polyethylene terephthalate (PET)/indium tin oxide (ITO) (PET/ITO) substrate conductive polymer showed positive outcomes for DSSCs. The PCE value for ZnO NPs was 2.198 ± 0.14 %, while for ZnO NPs/ITO NPs, it was 6.680 ± 0.12 %. This work achieved remarkable and competitive performance when compared to a solid (indium tin oxides/glass)-based substrate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Flexible dye sensitized solar cells (FDSSCs) are a potentially more economical, clean, efficient, easy-to-fabricate, and environmentally friendly photovoltaic (PV) technology than traditional silicon-based ones [1,2]. It may also be used as flexible window or wall devices in building-integrated photovoltaic systems [3]. The majority of FDSSCs are composed of dye and semiconductor oxides. Numerous semiconductor oxides [4], including SnO2, TiO2 [5,6], ZnO [7], and Nb2O5, have been used as an electron transport medium. Because of its special qualities, which include low cost, non-toxicity, and ease of synthesis, ZnO has proven to be an effective substitute for FDSSCs. Globally, nanotechnology is becoming more and more popular, finding utilizations in food, feed, pharmaceuticals, and personal hygiene products [8]. Nanoparticles are produced in comparatively high quantities using conventional procedures [9]. Due to their distinct characteristics, such particles have generated a lot of curiosity [10]. Nanoscale semiconductor materials [11] have novel properties because the quantum size effect or enormous surface area modifies their physical and chemical characteristics [12,13]. Of all the metal oxide nanomaterials currently accessible [13], zinc oxide Nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) [14] is an important material for research in a variety of applications. ZnO NPs hexagonal wurtzite structure [14] has garnered prominence as a luminescent material due to its structural [15], optical [16,17], thermal [17], electrical, and luminescence capabilities that can be used to make useful devices [18]. As nanomaterials' shape [19], size, and distribution-dependent capabilities, structure [17,20], or morphology [14,17] management, ZnO NPs have long been a priority in nano research [21]. ZnO NPs is useful because it is non-toxic to the environment [22] and can withstand radiation. The features of ZnO NPs materials are known to be affected by the production method, growth conditions, and post-growth treatments [23,24]. To prepare the ZnO nanostructure [25], lots of methodologies have been adopted. Epitaxy, pulsed laser deposition, thermal evaporations, solvothermal method, vapor phase production [26], precipitation, sol-gel, and sonochemical method are just few of the methods used to create ZnO nanostructures [26–28].

Of all the methods mentioned above, sol-gel is an excellent choice for producing ZnO NPs [29] due to its ease of modification and affordability [30,31]. The use of this process for nanoparticle production offers several advantages, including shorter production times and lower operating temperatures compared to alternative methods [32]. The effect of annealing, ZnO NPs generated using the sol-gel technique was reported by Omri et al. [33] To investigate the formation of ZnO nanostructures, Kumar et al. [34] employed a sol-gel method involving zinc sulfate heptahydrate and NaOH as precursors. It was found that the bandgap of ZnO nanostructures [7] can be lowered by increasing the calcination temperature, which in turn causes the maximum absorption to move to longer wavelengths [33]. The electrical and optical characteristics are highly correlated with the crystallite size and orientation, which are mostly influenced by the sizes of the NPs. There are still several challenges that must be overcome before effective ZnO NPs can be created. The first issue is characterising the residual n-type conductivity of ZnO NPs that have been inadvertently doped [35].

The development of Polymer [36] and lightweight solar energy [37] harvesting devices, such as dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [38], has garnered significant attention due to their potential for diverse applications. Commonly utilized polymeric substrates for conductive polymer substrates in DSSCs include polyethylene terephthalate (PET)/indium tin oxide (ITO) and polyethylene naphthalate (PEN)/ITO substrates [39,40]. In recent years, both substrates have incorporated coated ITO to enhance performance [41]. However, the thermal stability of these polymer substrates poses a critical limitation. Prior to incorporating non-flexible materials in DSSCs, the anode and cathode films undergo a heating process at 450 °C for 30 min to eliminate any residues and solvents from the precursor [42]. Unfortunately, PET/ITO and PEN/ITO polymer substrates have a maximum thermal stability threshold of around 150 °C [5,43]. Surpassing this temperature can lead to deformation of the flexible electrodes, making it essential to restrict the processing temperature to 150 °C or lower [44]. This low-temperature treatment, however, impedes the efficient decomposition of precursor residues, resulting in suboptimal DSSC performance. To address these challenges, this study focuses on the development of composite materials by combining zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) with indium tin oxide nanoparticles (ITO NPs). The aim is to explore the feasibility and effectiveness of utilizing ZnO/ITO composites in flexible dye-sensitized solar cells (FDSSCs). The potential enhancement in the performance and applicability of FDSSCs through the integration of ZnO/ITO composites could significantly impact the development of flexible electrode polymer substrates for lightweight solar energy harvesting devices. To optimize the efficiency of these composites for FDSSCs, which were fabricated using di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis-(isothiocyanato) bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato) ruthenium (II) (N719) dyes as sensitizers, composites of ITO NPs with ZnO NPs were created under carefully controlled conditions. The inclusion of ITO NPs, known for their good optical performance and wide range of applications, offers a cost-effective addition to the metal oxides used in these systems. ITO NPs have the potential to enhance electron transport and reduce charge recombination by functioning as energy donors. The objective of this research is to modify the structure and optical properties of ZnO NPs through hybridization with ITO NPs and to improve the performance of FDSSCs. This study examines the formation of a ZnO NPs/ITO NPs hybrid connection and its impact on the composite. Specifically, we investigate the effect of ITO NPs on the structure, optical properties, and performance of ZnO NPs in FDSSCs. The insights gained from this research are expected to contribute to the advancement of high-performance, flexible, and lightweight solar energy harvesting devices.

From Across Organic, we obtained zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn (CH3COO) 2.2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99 %), indium (III) chloride (InCl3. 4H2O), tin (IV) chloride (SnCl4. 5H2O), ethylenediamine (EDA) (99 %), ammonium hydroxide (NH3) solution, acetonitrile (99.5 %), and ethyl alcohol (99.5 %)

ce:section.ce:section-title: (A) Synthesized of ZnO NPs and the ZnO NPs/ITO NPs composite
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 M of zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn (CH3COO) 2 .2H2O (99 %) and 0.2 M of Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) dissolved separately in deionized water (DI) (1:1 ratio Zn (CH3COO).2H2O: NaOH). Then NaOH added to Zn (CH3COO) 2 .2H2O slowly dropwise vigorously stirring at room temperature. Then waiting up to a white transparent solution is formed. After the white transparent Zn(OH)2 precipitate was formed, insert it in an oven at 800C for 2hrs [45]. Centrifuge for 30 min at 3000 rpm using ethanol and DI water. Then calcined at 3000C–5000C. Then using the sol-gel method ZnO NPs were prepared successfully. A ZnO NPs/ITO NPs composite was prepared by just mixing appropriate ratios of ZnO nanoparticles (NPs) and ITO nanoparticles (NPs). The dispersion was stirred for 3 h. Subsequently, the composite was washed once with ethanol and then with deionized (DI) water before being dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: (B) Preparation of ITO nanoparticles by precipitation method
ce:section.ce:para: Indium(III) chloride (InCl3. 4H2O (2.6 g, 6.7 mmol) and Tin (IV) chloride (SnCl4. 5H2O (0.26 g, 0.74 mmol) was dissolved in 40 °C–50 °C DI water (10 mL) and the solution was stirred for 30 min. To regulate the dispersant, a 5 % Ammonia solution (1.4 ml) was added to the mixture solution and stirred for 10 min. The pH-9 was checked for the controlled acidity. Ethylene diamine (EDA) solution (0.76 ml) was added as the precipitant. The formed precipitation was washed with DI water and ethyl alcohol after being agitated for a set amount of time. After washing, the precipitant dried at 120 °C for 1hr. Then it was calcined at 450 °C–600 °C for 1hr to get ITO nanopowder.

Flexible electrodes Polyethylene terephthalate (PET)/ITO substrate, measuring 2 × 2 cm2, were cut into pieces and pre-cleaned using acetone, ethanol, and DI water and UV light irradiation in a vacuum for 5 min. Separately, dispersions of ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs/ITO NPs (40 mg each) in 200 μL of DI water were agitated for 4 h. The dispersions were then coated onto the pre-cleaned PET/ITO substrate using spin coating and annealed in an oven at 70 °C for 25 min. After the annealing process, the electrodes were submerged in an ethanol solution (5 mL) containing 0.5 mM N719 for 12 h. The construction of counter electrode involved coating the PET/ITO substrate with platinum (Pt) using electron sputtering.

In a separate process, the annealed electrodes were submerged in ethanol (5 mL) solutions of N719 (0.5 mM) for 12 h. The electrodes coated with ZnO NPs, and ZnO NPs/ITO NPs were immersed in a 1 M NaOH solution (5 mL) for 12 h to extract the dye loaded on the electrodes. Extra remaining dyes were removed using DI water. The amounts of dye in the extractants were calculated using calibration curves and the calorimetric method.

The ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs/ITO NPs photo-cathode films, with a thickness of 0.144 mm, on the PET/ITO substrate were assembled with the platinum (Pt)/PET/ITO substrate as counter electrode. Redox electrolytes (0.3 M KI, 0.05 M I2, and 10 mL of acetonitrile solvent) were added between the two constructed film electrodes on PET/ITO substrate. The I–V measurement was performed using a blue (430 nm) light-emitting diode (LED) light source with a ZAHNER, Xpot, 26356 German instrument. The total PCE (Photovoltaic Conversion Efficiency) was calculated using the equation below, based on the intensity of the incident light[46].
(1)
η =
J sc
V oc
P in
FF
Where
P
i n
is light incident power,
V oc
is the open-circuit voltage,
J sc
is the circuit current density and FF is the fill factor.

TEM images were used to characterize the size and shape of the created nanocomposites, specifically the nanoparticles of ZnO and ZnO NPs@ITO. The mean diameters measured for these nanoparticles were 15.22 ± 11 nm and 14.54 ± 16 nm, respectively, which align with previously published values [47]. These measurements can be found in Fig. 1 .

However, the presence of smaller nanoparticles was observed in a ZnO NPs@ITO NPs (10 wt%) composite. The findings indicate that the combination of ZnO NPs and ITO NPs of varying sizes results in attractive interactions between them. The beneficial properties of these nanoparticles are evident in TEM Fig. 1(a) and (b), where the ZnO NPs form hexagonal structures with sphere-like nanoparticles visible in certain regions. Different dimension sizes can be observed across all particle categories, but the average size for composite ZnO NPs and ITO NPs is approximately 12–16 nm. The sizes calculated using TEM are 15.22 ± 11 nm and 14.54 ± 16 nm, respectively, suggesting the possible formation of a heterojunction between them. Moreover, Fig. 1(a) and (b) illustrate that the incorporation of ITO NPs on top of ZnO NPs leads to an increase in surface roughness. This observation is supported by the size distribution histograms shown in Fig. 1(c) and (d) for ZnO NPs and ITO NPs, which confirm that most particles fall within the range calculated using TEM. SEM images of ZnO NPs in Fig. 1(e) and (f) display a uniform dispersion of hexagon-shaped nanoparticles. The SEM analysis also reveals the homogeneous and smooth surface morphology of the ZnO nanoparticles. However, in Fig. 1(f), a noticeable change in morphology is observed when the ZnO NPs are treated with ITO NPs. This change can be attributed to surface interactions between the two types of nanoparticles. Interactions between ZnO NPs and ITO NPs may lead to the adsorption or deposition of ITO NPs onto the surface of ZnO NPs, thereby modifying their surface properties and altering their morphology. These interactions may involve chemical reactions at the nanoparticle surfaces, potentially resulting in the formation of new compounds or surface modifications that impact the morphology of ZnO NPs. The specific reasons for these morphological changes depend on experimental conditions, nanoparticle characteristics, and interaction mechanisms between ZnO and ITO NPs in each study mentioned. Further analysis and characterization techniques are necessary to determine the exact mechanisms responsible for the observed changes.

The EDX pattern displayed in Fig. 1(g) confirms the presence of zinc (Zn) and oxygen (O) as the sole elements detected in the sample, implying the absence of any other trace elements in the synthesized nanopowder. The atomic and weight percentages of the components in ZnO NPs indicate that the particles consist entirely of Zn and O. This discovery supports the nearly equal atomic ratio of Zn and O depicted in Fig. 1(g), demonstrating that the quantities of these elements involved in the reaction or compound formation of ZnO are as shown. Additionally, Fig. 1(h) presents the EDS analysis, which reveals the elemental components of the collective part to include oxygen (O), zinc (Zn), tin (Sn), and indium (In). This suggests the presence of ZnO and indium tin oxide (ITO). The weight percentage (wt%) and atomic percentage (At%) values for each element in Table 1 further substantiate this finding. Additionally, an unexpected element, carbon (C), was detected. This carbon presence is likely attributable to impurities.

Table 1 presents data on the elemental composition (in weight percent and atomic percent) and band gap of ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs/ITO NPs. The composition of ZnO NPs predominantly consists of Zn and O, with Zn accounting for approximately 59.99 wt% and 63.24 at%, and O accounting for about 48.01 wt% and 36.76 at%. In the case of ZnO/ITO, in addition to Zn and O, there are additional elements present, namely Tin (Sn) and Indium (In), indicating the incorporation of ITO NPs into the ZnO NPs matrix. Carbon (C) is also observed [36], likely as a contaminant (Fig. 1 (h)). The band gap of ZnO NPs is measured at different annealing temperatures (300 °C, 350 °C, 400 °C, 450 °C, and 500 °C). The band gap decreases as the annealing temperature increases, indicating a change in the electronic structure of the material. For ZnO NPs with different wt% of ITO NPs: The band gap is measured at 450 °C for ZnO NPs with varying weight percentages (5 wt%, 10 wt%, and 15 wt%) of ITO NPs. The band gap decreases with increasing wt% of ITO NPs up to 10 wt% and then increases slightly at 15 wt% (seen in Table 1).

The inclusion of ITO NPs into the ZnO NPs matrix results in modifications to the elemental composition and impacts the band gap of the composite material. The band gap of ZnO NPs is found to be influenced by the annealing temperature, highlighting the significance of thermal treatment in fine-tuning the electronic properties of the material. Moreover, the band gap of the ZnO/ITO composite displays variability in accordance with the concentration of ITO NPs, indicating potential utility in optoelectronic devices that require precise manipulation of the band gap.

As depicted in Fig. 2 (A) and Table 2 (A) , the X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak values of the ZnO NP samples obtained through the precipitation synthesis method at different annealing temperatures correspond to the d-spacing, indices, and 2theta angles. The subsequent sections of the table provide the same index information for the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), and (201) planes at 2θ = 31.73°, 34.42°, 36.26°, 47.53°, 56.53°, 62.87°, 66.35°, 67.86°, 69.04°, respectively, for the ZnO NP obtained at an annealing temperature of 450 °C. The same indices are also provided for annealing temperatures of 300 °C, 350 °C, 400 °C, and 500 °C, consistent with the findings of previous researchers [48]. This confirms the successful preparation of pure crystalline ZnO NPs. The desired levels of crystallinity and purity of the ZnO NPs were achieved within the temperature range of 300–500 °C. However, at an annealing temperature of 450 °C, the d-spacing values and 2theta values closely match the standard reported values. Analysis of the diffraction patterns of the ZnO NPs reveals characteristic peaks corresponding to specific crystallographic planes within the hexagonal structure of ZnO. These peaks can be indexed using the JCPDS card No. 01-079-2205 [49], enabling researchers to identify and confirm the crystal structure of the ZnO nanoparticles. The XRD peak diffraction of the ITO NPs, exhibiting a cubic-like structure (JCPDS 06–0416) [50], is observed at 2θ = 21.77°, 30.74°, 35.72°, 37.90°, 42.21°, 46.04°, 51.35°, 56.27°, 59.55°, and 60.91° for the planes (211), (222), (400), (411), (332), (431), (440), (433), (611), and (622), respectively. Furthermore, it is noted that the intensity of the ZnO NP peaks increases with increasing temperature up to 450 °C. This temperature-dependent increase in intensity suggests that as the temperature rises, the atoms within the ZnO NPs rearrange themselves into a more ordered lattice, leading to enhanced crystallinity. However, when the temperature exceeds 500 °C, a decrease in the intensity of all diffraction peaks is observed. This decrease implies a reduction in X-ray scattering, which may be attributed to a decline in the crystalline quality of the ZnO nanoparticles. At higher temperatures, factors such as grain growth, lattice defects, or phase transformations can contribute to a decrease in crystalline quality and, consequently, a decrease in peak intensity.
The details provided in Fig. 2 (A) would show the variation in peak intensities as a function of temperature, visually representing the changes in crystallinity and the decreasing peak intensities.

Particle sizes (D), from Scherrer formula, using the formula for a hexagonal system was calculated using the following:
D
(
n m
)
=
0.94 λ
β
cos
θ

The calculated particle size uing above Scherrer formla was
D
(
n m
)
=
14.58 ± 0.24 and
D
(
n m
)
= 14.31 ± 0.37 for ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs/ITO NPS respectively. Since the mean diameter particle size calculated from the TEM 15.22 ± 0.11 nm was for ZnO NPs, while 14.54 ± 0.16 nm was for ZnO NPs/ITO NPs, then the particle size from TEM image and from XRD data were almost consistence, This demonstrates that the particle sizes derived from TEM images and those calculated using X-ray Diffraction (XRD) data are almost consistent, with only minor discrepancies between the two sets of measurements.

As depicted in Fig. 2 (B) and Table 2(B), the XRD peaks and peak positions of ZnO nanoparticles (NPs) exhibit changes corresponding to the addition of indium tin oxide (ITO) NPs. This illustrates the influence of ITO NPs on the structure of ZnO NPs. The composite of ZnO NPs/ITO NPs clearly contains all the known XRD peaks of ITO NPs, along with their associated crystallographic planes. Nevertheless, a shift in the peak positions of ZnO NPs is observed following the incorporation of ITO NPs. For instance, the peak positions of 31.73 °C, 34.42 °C, and 36.26 °C for ZnO NPs are shifted to 31.70 °C, 34.37 °C, and 36.29 °C, respectively, after the addition of ITO NPs. This suggests that ITO NPs can influence the crystallography of ZnO NPs, potentially enabling their application in other areas. The lattice parameters for ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs@ITO NPs, calculated based on the XRD peaks, are determined to be 3.124 Å and 4.642 Å, respectively, and 3.214 Å and 4.725 Å, respectively.

As observed in UV–Vis Fig. 3 (A) and (B), the spectra of ZnO NPs exhibit the highest peaks at 380 nm, regardless of the annealing temperatures used. The redshifted wavelength of ZnO NPs, however, becomes noticeable only at certain temperatures. The actual band gap of ZnO NPs shifts along with the temperature, but the redshift wavelength with the new band spectra of ZnO NPs is observed at 428 nm after the addition of 10 wt% mass of ITO NPs to ZnO NPs (as shown in Fig. 3 (B)). This shift can be attributed to the electron transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) at the energy level. The influence of ITO NPs on the shift of wavelength from the ultraviolet band (380 nm) to the visible band (428 nm) in the spectra is possible. Although exposure to visible light does not pose any danger to humans, the composite of these materials may have multiple applications in various contexts, such as photocatalytic degradation, solar energy, and similar processes.

The tauc plots depicted in Fig. 3 (C), (E) and Table 1 of ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs/ITO NPs demonstrate the variation of the band gap of ZnO NPs at different annealing temperatures and the band gap of ZnO NPs at different weight percentages (wt%) of ITO NPs. Consequently, as the temperature is raised from 300 °C to 450 °C, the band gap of the materials decreases, but it increases after 450 °C. This indicates that the annealing temperature can influence the band gap of the materials, which correspond to their conductivity. As illustrated in Fig. 3(D) and Table 1, a small band gap is observed at 450 °C. Additionally, when ZnO NPs are treated with different wt% of ITO NPs, the change in the band gap of ZnO NPs at 10 wt% of ITO NPs reduces from 2.94 eV to 2.88 eV (as shown in Fig. 3(E) and (F) and Table 1). The interaction between ZnO and ITO NPs can result in chemical reactions or alloying at the interface, which can introduce new energy levels or modify the electronic structure of the ZnO NPs, thereby affecting the band gap. The presence of ITO NPs, which typically have a lower band gap than ZnO, can influence the overall band gap of the nanocomposite. Additionally, dopants can create energy levels within the band gap, thereby influencing its width. The nature and concentration of defects or dopants can vary with the annealing temperature or the weight percentage of ITO NPs, leading to changes in the band gap.

Fig. 4 shows the deconvoluted line spectra of the peaks for ZnO NPs (O 1s, Zn2P) and ZnO NPs NPs@ITO NP (O 1s, Zn2P). The XPS fine spectra in Fig. 4 (A), (B), and (D) reveal differences in the binding energies and area intensities of the peaks. The binding energies of the O 1s peaks for ZnO NPs decreased from 532.02 eV to 533.6 eV–531.34 eV and 533.21 eV after the addition of ITO NPs, indicating an interaction between ZnO NPs and ITO NPs. This shift in binding energies suggests a chemical interaction, possibly due to surface interactions or electronic effects. The presence of ZnO NPs is confirmed by the strong double peaks of Zn 2p (Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2) [51], which are associated with Zn–O and Zn–OH bonds, respectively. The area intensities of the O 1s peaks for ZnO NPs/ITO NPs were lower compared to those of ZnO NPs, indicating a replacement of oxygen species on the surface after ITO NP coating. In summary, the XPS results demonstrate the chemical and structural changes caused by coating ZnO NPs with ITO NPs, as evidenced by shifts in binding energies and changes in peak intensities of the O 1s and Zn 2p spectra. These changes indicate the integration of ITO NPs onto the surface of ZnO NPs, influencing their properties and potentially enhancing their functionality for various applications.

During the process of excitation, a material absorbs photons with specific energy [13], which causes electrons to transition from the valence band to the conduction band or to higher energy states within the band gap. As a result, electron holes are created in the valence band. When a material emits light after absorbing photons, it is referred to as photoluminescence. Fig. 5 (A) illustrates the photoluminescence (PL) profiles of ZnO nanoparticles (NPs) at various temperatures: 300, 350, 400, 450, and 500 °C. These PL profiles provide insight into the emission of light at specific wavelengths, thereby revealing the presence of different energy levels and defects within the material. In the case of ZnO NPs, the broad emission band is a result of the overlay of a major peak and a broad emission, with the peak occurring at approximately 524 nm when annealed at 450 °C. This signifies that the optical characteristics of photoluminescence [52]can be influenced by the annealing temperature. As depicted in Fig. 5 (A), five distinct peaks were observed at 498 nm, 506 nm, 510 nm, 515 nm, and 523 nm for ZnO NPs annealed at 300 °C, 500 °C, 350 °C, 400 °C, and 450 °C, respectively. Various defects, including oxygen intermediate (Oi), zinc interstitial (Zni), oxygen vacancies (Vo), zinc vacancies (Vzn), and oxygen antisite (Ozn), were considered as potential sources of the deep level emission observed within the visible range. Furthermore, Gaussian fitting [53], was utilized to examine the broad spectrum in the visible region and investigate the blue (470 nm and 492 nm) and green (522 nm) emissions.

The blue fluorescence at 498 nm was generated through the recombination of zinc interstitial (Zni) energy level and zinc vacancy (VZn). The blue fluorescence peaks at 498 nm were reported by Lakshi Saikia et al. to the transfer of electrons from the upper valence band to the zinc interstitial (Zni) site [54]. Oxygen vacancies, created at the deep donor level in the material's bandgap, are responsible for the 523 nm green emission peak. Whenever the temperature of the substrate rises, the visible-light intensity falls, demonstrating that the substrate temperature plays a significant role in the creation of different defects throughout the lattice of ZnO NPs.

The CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram (from Fig. 5 (B) above) visually represents the colors perceived by the human eye. It demonstrates the range of colors that can be achieved by varying combinations of the primary colors: red, green, and blue. This diagram is particularly useful for studying the color properties of materials, such as ZnO nanoparticles (NPs), under different conditions, including various annealing temperatures. Annealing temperatures significantly impact the photoluminescence (PL) properties of ZnO NPs [55], which, in turn, affect their position on the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. Data collected from ZnO NPs at different annealing temperatures have revealed specific (x, y) chromaticity coordinates, which indicate the color output of the NPs. For instance, at an annealing temperature of 450 °C, the ZnO NPs have chromaticity coordinates of (x, y) = (0.28, 0.50), placing them in the yellowish-green region of the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. At an annealing temperature of 400 °C, the ZnO NPs have chromaticity coordinates of (x, y) = (0.25, 0.44), positioning them in the green region of the diagram. These specific chromaticity coordinates suggest that the ZnO NPs emit light within the visible spectrum, particularly in the green to yellowish-green range, depending on the annealing temperature. The ability of these ZnO NPs to emit light in the yellowish-green and green regions makes them suitable for use in visible lamps. Visible lamps that emit light in these color ranges have various applications, including indoor lighting, display technologies, and decorative lighting. Furthermore, the efficiency and stability of ZnO NPs at these temperatures make them highly promising for such applications. Additionally, ZnO NPs can be utilized in solar cells due to their ability to absorb and emit light within the visible spectrum. The specific chromaticity coordinates of ZnO NPs indicate efficient light emission, which can enhance the performance of solar cells by improving light absorption and conversion efficiency. Consequently, this can lead to improved energy harvesting and increased power output in solar cell technologies.

Fig. 5 (C) displays the PL spectra obtained from our experiments to examine the impact of ITO (indium tin oxide) nanoparticles on the optical characteristics of ZnO NPs/ITO NPs. We investigated using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, which allows us to study the emission of light from the materials under specific conditions. In this study, we recorded the PL spectra of a composite of ZnO NPs and ITO NPs (ITO NPs/ZnO NPs) materials at low temperatures ranging from 84K to 300K.The low temperature photoluminescence measurements and optical transmittance analysis experiments were carried out at an optimized temperature of 450 °C. In this Fig. 5 (C), the low temperature photoluminescence characteristics of the ZnO NPs@ITO NPs nanostructures, we observe a single emitting band, which corresponds to a broad visible emission peak centered around 535 nm. This emission is observed within the low temperature range of 84K–300K for the ZnO NPs@ITO NPs hybrid nanostructure. In summary. these findings provide valuable insights into the optical behavior of the hybrid nanostructures and contribute to our understanding of the interaction between ITO NPs and ZnO NPs.

It is evident that the band edge emission increases in the sample treated at 84K compared to 300K. This increase can be attributed to the lower temperature treatment, which reduces impurities in the material and reveals the photoluminescence characteristics of the semiconductor. On the other hand, the intensity of the broad emission decreases significantly from 84K to 300K. This indicates that room temperature can affect the photoluminescence behavior of the material. However, when the bare ZnO NPs@ITO NPs sample is excited by a laser source and its electrons are stimulated to the conduction band, only a small portion of these electrons return directly to the valence band, while the majority become trapped in the defect levels. Consequently, the electrons from the conduction band are easily trapped back into the defect states, resulting in a broad hump in the visible region.

The impact of ITO NPs on FDSSC performance has not been assessed yet, but it effectively produces a quick photocurrent response. As shown in Fig. 6 and Table 3 , loading the optimized ITO NPs (10 wt%) onto the Pt/PET/ITO substrate enhances the current density from 7.65 + 0.13 to 11.420 ± 0.14 mA/cm2. As a result, the PCE increases from 2.198 ± 0.14 to 6.680 + 0.12 %. The interaction between ITO NPs and ZnO NPs augments charge separation, decreases charge recombination, and boosts charge transport at ZnO NPs. These factors are deemed crucial for enhancing photocurrent production. Table 3 provides data on the I–V performance of FDSSCs with ZnO NP@ITO NP photocathodes at different weight percentages of ITO NPs loaded on ZnO NPs. This data reveals a trend where the photocurrent density (Isc) and efficiency (η%) increase with higher weight percentages of ITO NPs, up to a certain point. This indicates that the incorporation of ITO NPs enhances the performance of the solar cells, but there may be an optimal weight percentage beyond which further loading does not significantly improve performance. Fig. 6(A) displays the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of FDSSCs with ZnO NPs at different weight percentages of ITO NPs. It shows that the PCE varies, indicating that the interaction between the surface of ZnO NPs and ITO NPs differs depending on the weight percentage of ITO NPs. This variation in interaction leads to differences in the performance of the FDSSCs, highlighting the importance of optimizing the weight percentage of ITO NPs for maximum efficiency.

Fig. 6 (B) (a), (b), (c), and (d) illustrate the I–V parameters at various mass loadings of indium tin oxide nanoparticles (ITO NPs). The data indicates that at a ratio of 10 wt% of zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) to ITO NPs, the dye loading efficiency is significantly higher. This observation implies that at this specific weight percentage, there is an optimal level of light absorption and performance, leading to improved efficiency. Consequently, these findings suggest that the performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) is influenced by the weight percentage of ITO NPs deposited on ZnO NPs. Therefore, it is crucial to optimize this weight percentage in order to maximize efficiency, as it directly impacts the interaction between the nanoparticles and ultimately determines the overall performance of the solar cells. Overall, the results indicate that the incorporation of ITO NPs into DSSCs results in notable enhancements in their performance, primarily attributed to improved charge transport and reduced recombination, ultimately leading to higher efficiency.

Table 4 presents a comparison of the performance of different flexible electrodes for DSSCs using various materials. The table includes information on the materials, deposition techniques, working electrode (WE), counter electrode (CE), power conversion efficiency (PCE) in %, and related references. The study reported a significant improvement in the performance of FDSSCs when ITO NPs were added to ZnO NPs, with the ZnO NPs/ITO NPs DSSC showing an approximate 6.680 ± 0.12 nearly double improvement.

Furthermore, the combination of ZnO NPs solar cells, N719 dye, and ITO NPs had a synergistic effect, leading to improved performance of the DSSCs. ITO NPs have been found to be excellent electron donors and acceptors. This study clearly demonstrates the dual functionality of photo-excited ITO NPs as both electron donors and acceptors. ITO nanoparticles (NPs) are widely known for their remarkable abilities in photo-induced electron transfer and light harvesting, which enhance their photocatalytic and light-energy conversion capabilities. The presence of ITO NPs improves photocurrent production, dye adsorption, light harvesting, PCE efficiency, recombination reduction, and mobility enhancement. Lastly, the use of ITO NPs holds the potential for achieving photogenerated charge separation.

The electrical characteristics of ZnO thin films are just as important as their optical characteristics for their performance. Fig. 7 demonstrates the impact of the amount of doping ITO NPs on the electrical resistivity of ZnO thin films. Specifically, it shows how the doping level (ITO NPs) affects the evolution of the electrical resistivity of ZnO/ITO NPs films. As the doping percentage increases, the resistivity of the samples decreases from 90.5 × 10−4 Ω cm (undoped ZnO NPs) and reaches its lowest value of 2.6 × 10−4 Ω cm for 10 % doping of ITO NPs. It then increases to 3.5 × 10−4 Ω cm (15 % ITO NPs). This drop in resistivity with increasing doping dose can be attributed to the increased quantity of charge mobilities (electrons) from donors In3+ ions integrated in the interstitial or substitutional sites of Zn2+ cations.

In summary, composites of ITO NPs and ZnO NPs were synthesized by combining the appropriate ratio of ITO NPs with ZnO NPs. Separately, ITO NPs and ZnO NPs do not exhibit a photovoltaic effect polymer-based DSSCs value due to rapid charge recombination in the ZnO semiconductor. However, when an adequate amount of ITO NPs is combined with ZnO NPs, the PCE performance of ZnO NPs in DSSCs improves synergicaly due to abundant charge separation and reduced charge recombination caused by the reduced band gap of the composite. Furthermore, the N719 adsorption of the composites increases after the addition of ITO NPs. It should be noted that ITO NPs primarily play a sensitizing role in the DSSC, while N719 acts as an accelerator of positive electron transfer and a restrainer of electron-hole recombination. This represents the distinctive sensitizing mechanism of the incorporating ITO NPs with N719-sensitized n-type solar cells. In our present investigation, a DSSC fabricated using a composite of ZnO NPs with ITO NPs using N719 achieved a PCE of 6.68 % at an ITO NPs content of 10 wt%. Therefore, it is evident that ITO NPs have a significant impact on both n-type DSSCs and the improvement of DSSC device performance, specifically in terms of PCE enhancement. Among different types of solar cells, DSSCs have a low PCE and short lifetime. However, they offer the advantages of low cost, compactness, facile production, and easy and inexpensive repair following breakage. Hence, the unique requirements for ITO NPs sensitizer-DSSCs suggest that further improvements can be expected.
This research received no external Funding.

Habtamu Fekadu Etefa: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Francis Birhanu Dejene: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Walter Sisulu University (WSU) for its support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and characterization of indium doped tin oxide (ITO) via a non-aqueous sol-gel

ACS Omega
